coredata.dc:title: Starch-based C-dots from natural Gadong tuber as pH fluorescence label for optical biosensing of arginine
coredata.dc:creator: Sonthanasamy, Regina Sisika A.
Lazim, Azwan Mat
Zuki, Siti Nur Syazni Mohd
Quay, Doris Huai Xia
Tan, Ling Ling
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence bi-enzyme optical arginine biosensor based on novel fluorophore carbon nanodots (C-dots) pH indicator derived from natural Gadong (Dioscorea hispida) tuber was developed. The brightly luminescent pH sensitive carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) exploited from exotic wild yam polysaccharides via aqueous-based synthesis method were immobilized on the acrylic-N-acryloxysuccinimidecopolymer microspheres. Recombinant Glaciozyma antarctica arginase (GaArg) cloned in E. coli expression system and commercial urease were grafted on the acrylic microspheres (AMS) via peptide covalent link. During fluorescence arginine detection, the immobilized arginase catalyzed hydrolysis conversion of arginine to ornithine and urea, and subsequent enzymatic hydrolysis of urea by immobilized urease rendering deprotonation of starch-based C-dots, resulted in the heightening of C-dots’ photoluminescence intensity due to elevated localized pH. Under 380 nm excitation, the C-dots fluorescence intensity at 420 nm increased linearly with arginine over the concentration range of 1–10 mM, with the limit of detection (LOD) estimated at 0.9 mM. The Gadong starch-based C-dots, which prepared from different natural tuber samples showed high reproducible physico-chemical properties, and gave reproducible fluorescence intensity at different pH values with relative standard deviation (RSD) values obtained at <1.0%, which suggests high reproducibility of the synthesis of starch-based luminecent C-dots. The optical enzymatic biosensor doped with C-dots pH label demonstrated high selectivity towards fluorescence biorecognition of arginine compared to some other amino acids normally present in packaged fruit juices. No significant difference was found between C-dots-labeled enzymatic fluorescence microsensor and HPLC standard method for arginine content detection in fruit juice samples of grapes, mangoes and oranges.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arginine
Biosensor
Carbon nanodot
Gadong starch
pH fluorescent label

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Production of Glaciozyma antarctica recombinant arginase
Synthesis of starch-based fluorescent carbon nanodots
Preparation of C-dots-based enzymatic fluorescence arginine biosensor
Optimization of C-dots loading, response time and linear response range
Arginine biosensor’s long term stability and interference studies
Detection of arginine concentration in fruit juices with C-dots-labeled bi-enzyme fluorescence microsensor and validation with HPLC standard method
Production of recombinant arginase and enzyme activity
Characterization of carbon nanodots synthesized from natural polysaccharides
Effect of C-dots concentration, arginine detection time and dynamic linear range
The lifetime and selectivity of luminescent C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor
Application of fluorescence enzymatic microbiosensor based on C-dots pH labeling agent for arginine concentration quantification in fresh fruit juices
Comparison of analytical performance of carbon dots-labeled enzymatic biosensor with other reported arginine biosensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent arginine biosensor design based on highly luminescent pH sensitive C-dots from native D. hispida starch and AMS copolymer microspheres as enzyme immobilization substrate.
TEM image of fluorescent C-dots synthesized from D. hispida starch nanocrystals captured by Philips TEM at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV.

Effect of pH on the fluoresecence response of C-dots by using 20 mM K-phosphate buffer at pH 8.0 added with different amounts of 0.1 M NaOH. The inset shows the pH intensity of C-dots as a function of pH variation between pH 8.0 and pH 11.0.

Fluorescence intensity of C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor as a function of C-dot loading from 0.005 mg μL−1 to 0.050 mg μL−1 under 380 nm excitation, and the presence of 10 mM arginine in 20 mM K-phosphate buffer at pH 8.0. AMS and enzyme-modified AMS samples were used as negative controls.

The photoluminescence response trend of the C-dots-based enzymatic arginine biosensor within arginine concentration range of 1–100 mM. The inset shows the dynamic linear range of fluorescence biosensor between 1 nM and 10 mM arginine at 420 nm emission wavelength when the excitation is performed at a wavelength of 380 nm, (n = 3).
Statistical comparison between C-dots-based fluorescence enzymatic biosensor and HPLC reference method for arginine concentration determination in squeezed grape, mango and orange juices (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *t critical value = 2.776 at 95% confidence level and 4 degrees of freedom.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of analytical performance of carbon dots-labeled bienzyme arginine fluorescence biosensor with recently reported optical and electrochemical arginine biosensors in terms of linear calibration range, long term stability and response time.

row.entry: Pomegrahate peel extract stabilized gold nanoparticles

head.ce:title: Starch-based C-dots from natural Gadong tuber as pH fluorescence label for optical biosensing of arginine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Advanced Materials and Renewable Resources, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM Bangi, Selangor Darul Ehsan, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Advanced Materials and Renewable Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Southest Asia Disaster Preventation Research Initiative (SEADPRI-UKM), Institute for Environment and Development (LESTARI), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM Bangi, Selangor Darul Ehsan, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southest Asia Disaster Preventation Research Initiative (SEADPRI-UKM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Southest Asia Disaster Preventation Research Initiative (SEADPRI-UKM), Institute for Environment and Development (LESTARI), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM Bangi, Selangor Darul Ehsan, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biosciences and Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM Bangi, Selangor Darul Ehsan, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biosciences and Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM Bangi, Selangor Darul Ehsan, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence bi-enzyme optical arginine biosensor based on novel fluorophore carbon nanodots (C-dots) pH indicator derived from natural Gadong (Dioscorea hispida) tuber was developed. The brightly luminescent pH sensitive carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) exploited from exotic wild yam polysaccharides via aqueous-based synthesis method were immobilized on the acrylic-N-acryloxysuccinimidecopolymer microspheres. Recombinant Glaciozyma antarctica arginase (GaArg) cloned in E. coli expression system and commercial urease were grafted on the acrylic microspheres (AMS) via peptide covalent link. During fluorescence arginine detection, the immobilized arginase catalyzed hydrolysis conversion of arginine to ornithine and urea, and subsequent enzymatic hydrolysis of urea by immobilized urease rendering deprotonation of starch-based C-dots, resulted in the heightening of C-dots’ photoluminescence intensity due to elevated localized pH. Under 380 nm excitation, the C-dots fluorescence intensity at 420 nm increased linearly with arginine over the concentration range of 1–10 mM, with the limit of detection (LOD) estimated at 0.9 mM. The Gadong starch-based C-dots, which prepared from different natural tuber samples showed high reproducible physico-chemical properties, and gave reproducible fluorescence intensity at different pH values with relative standard deviation (RSD) values obtained at <1.0%, which suggests high reproducibility of the synthesis of starch-based luminecent C-dots. The optical enzymatic biosensor doped with C-dots pH label demonstrated high selectivity towards fluorescence biorecognition of arginine compared to some other amino acids normally present in packaged fruit juices. No significant difference was found between C-dots-labeled enzymatic fluorescence microsensor and HPLC standard method for arginine content detection in fruit juice samples of grapes, mangoes and oranges.

ce:section.ce:para: In food and beverage industry, determination of arginine concentrations is an important factor for monitoring of product quality especially wines and juices. In winery bioprocess, arginine reduces to urea and ornithine by the arginase activity [1]. Some urea is absorbed by yeast, and some is released into the fermentation medium. It is not appropriate to use high levels of arginine content of grapes to produce wine as the accumulated urea in the fermentation medium can spontaneously react with ethanol to form ethyl carbamate, which is a potent carcinogen [2,3]. To circumvent this possible health hazard, detection of arginine levels in fruit juices is necessary. The differences of the arginine concentrations in wine and fruit juices may determine the urea and thus the ethyl carbamate levels of the fermented product to certain extent. However, there is no safe level for arginine consumption or known self-limiting intakes of fruit juices [4].

There are several conventional analytical methods typically used for evaluation of arginine content in samples. Chinard's reaction and Sakaguchi reaction are known as the traditional chemical assays for colorimetric determination of arginine involving the use of optically reactive compounds, such as α-naphthol, sodium hypochlorite, sodium hypobromite, oxine, etc. The greatest drawbacks in these colorimetric assays are the slow rate of colour development and the colours fade out immediately after the chemical reaction, and almost disappeared in 10 min [5]. Whereas, examples of instrumental methods commonly employed for quantitative determination of arginine are UV–Vis spectrophotometry [6], capillary electrophoresis, ion exchange chromatography, liquid chromatography and gas chromatography. Arginine determination based on chromatography method should be followed by fluorescence or electrochemical detection using pre- or post-column derivatives, and that majority of the conventional analytical procedures being undertaken for arginine determination are still a labourintensiveundertaking, time-consuming and requiring sample pre-treatment to avoid inhibition by eliminating toxic compounds and toxic intermediates formed [7].

Many quantum dot (QD) core metals e.g. Pb, Hg, Se and Cd are known to be toxic to vertebrate systems although they offer invaluable societal benefits, such as biomedical imaging, targeted drug deliveryandcancer therapy. The synthesis and versatile applications of heavy metal-based QDs in medical and biological research, however, has slowly reduced as the introduction of carbon-based QDs become the substitution candidates [8]. The discovery of quantum-based carbon with smaller size opens up a new path for fluorescence-based assays. Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracting considerable attention since the discovery of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with less than 10 nm size in 2004. C-dot is a carbon-based material that generally composed of a large amount of sp 2 hybridized carbon with relatively low amount of oxygen-, hydrogen- and nitrogen-containing residues on the surface [9]. The oxygen and hydrogen moieties of C-dots could form hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which could facilitate surface functionalization and improve hydrophilicity of C-dots. C-dots possess fascinating properties, such as excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, outstanding water solubility, tunable fluorescence emission and excitation as well as high quantum yield (QY) [10]. C-dots have been used for fluorescence assay of various metal ions, DNA and protein assays owing to their extraordinary electron transfer properties, and exhibited as excellent donors of fluorescent resonance energy transmitters (FRET). Quenching of fluorescence may occur through energy transfer, charge diverting and surface absorption [11]. In view of the great fluorescence properties of C-dots over organic dyes, they have been widely applied as excellent fluorescent markers for biosensing, cellular imaging and medical diagnosis [12].

In this study, native Gadong starch-derived C-dots have been synthesized via acid hydrolysis followed by acid dehydration and surface oxidation steps with Gadong tuber as the natural precursor material, for later adopted as pH sensitive fluorescence probe in the enzymatic biosensing of arginine concentration. Glaciozyma antarctica (G. antarctica) is a psychrophilic yeast found in the sea-ice water at the Antartic region [13]. An arginase from the psychrophilic yeast, G. Antarctica was subcloned and expressed as a recombinant enzyme in E. coli expression system, and co-immobilized with commercial urease on the acrylic-N-acryloxysuccinimidecopolymer microspheres via peptide covalent bond. Arginases are metalloenzymes that catalyse convertion of L-arginine into L-ornithine and urea. As arginine undergoes cleavage via immobilized arginase to form urea and ornithine, subsequent enzymatic hydrolysis of urea leads to deprotonation of immobilized C-dots, and increases fluorescence response of C-dots-based bienzymatic biosensor as a result of generation of hydroxide ion that raises the microenvironment pH at biosensor-solution interface. The schematic representation of C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor by co-immobilization of C-dots pH indicator, arginase (Arg) and urease (Ur) on the acrylic microspheres (AMS) for fluorescence bioassay of arginine concentration is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

All chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Amino acid such as citrulline, serine and lysine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Anhydrous acetone was purchased from ACROS, USA. Ultrapure water (~18.2MΩ.cm, 25 °C) was acquired from a water purifying system (Sartorius, Model Arium®611DI), and was used to prepared all the chemical and biological solutions. Arginine solution was prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of arginine powder (Duchefa Biochemie, >98.5%) in 20 mM potassium phosphate (K-phosphate) buffer at pH 8.0. Urease (type III from Jack Beans, SIGMA) solution was prepared by dissolving 2 mg of enzyme in 1000 µL of 20 mM K-phosphate buffer (pH 8.0), and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C until further use. Glaciozyma antarctica, E. coli competent cell strains DH5α and BL21 (DE3) were obtained from Molecular Mycology Laboratory, School of Biosciences and Biotechnology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia.

The size and morphology of polyacrylate microspheres and carbon nanodots (C-dots) were determined by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Merlin/Merlin Compact/Supra 55VP) and transmission electron microscopy (Philips, 300 kV), respectively. The functional groups present on the C-dots and acrylic microspheres (AMS) were characterized by Agilent Cary 630 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, whilst UV–Vis absorption of carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) was examined with UV-2450 SHIMADZU UV–Vis spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction pattern of C-dots synthesized from Gadong starch nanoparticles was captured by Bruker/A8 Advance diffractometer with CuKα radiation (Ni filter) operating at the current power of 40 kV and 40 mA. Fluorescence properties of C-dots was analyzed by using LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). Recombinant arginase enzyme activity based on urea absorption level was performed using Microplate Reader Infinite M200 machine (Tecan, Austria). Agilent1200 series high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system was used to validate the performance of c-dots-based bi-enzyme fluorescence biosensor for arginine determination in fruit juice samples

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of Glaciozyma antarctica recombinant arginase
ce:section.ce:para: A recombinant G. antarctica arginase (GaArg) was subcloned in E. coli expression system. The pET30 Ek/LIC plasmid harbouring GaArg gene was transformed into E. coli DH5α cloning host and grown on Luria-Bertani (LB) agar containing 50 µg mL−1 kanamycin. The plasmid was extracted and GaArg sequence was validated by DNA sequencing (1st Base, Malaysia). Subsequently, the plasmid was transformed into E. coli BL21 (DE3) expression host and grown on LB agar containing 50 µg mL−1 kanamycin for recombinant protein expression. A single colony was picked using a sterile toothpick and inoculated in 5 mL LB broth with 50 µg mL−1 kanamycin and grown at 37 °C, 250 rpm for overnight and used as the starter culture. Then, the starter culture cells were transferred into a fresh 500 mL LB broth with 50 µg mL−1 kanamycin at the final concentration of 1% and grown until the cell density reached an OD600 of 0.8. Isopropyl-β-D-thiogalactoside (IPTG) of 0.1 mM was added into the cell culture to maintain protein induction at 16 °C, 250 rpm for 18 h. The cell pellet was collected upon centrifugation at 4500 × g for 30 min. Then, the cell pellet was resuspended in 40 mL lysis buffer, pH 8.0 (0.1 M Tris–HCl; 0.5 M NaCl; 2 mM β-mercaptoethanol; 2 mg mL−1 lysozyme; 40 µL protease inhibitor; 5 mM MnSO4) and the cells were lysed on ice using 1 mL of Bug Buster® Protein Extraction Reagent (Novagen, USA) and homogenizer (Whirlimixer, UK) set at 35% (amplitude at 3 min for 3 cycles). Unbroken cells and cell debris were removed by centrifugation at 12,000g for 1 h. The supernatant was collected and analysed by SDS-PAGE. Protein concentration was determined by Bradford method (Amresco, USA) using Bovine Serum Albumin as the standard.

The recombinant arginase enzyme activity was determined by estimating the amount of urea produced from the hydrolysis of arginine. The amount of urea generated in the reaction was measured as described by [14]. The recombinant GaArg hydrolyses arginine into urea and ornithine in 15 mM glycine-NaOH buffer, pH 9.0. The enzyme reaction is shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
Arginine
+
H 2
O
→ Arginase
Ornithine
+
Urea

The urea standard curve was performed using 0.1–1.0 μmol urea. Arginase activity was assessed in a reaction containing 10 μg mL−1 recombinant GaArg, 10 mM arginine as the substrate and 15 mM glycine-NaOH buffer (pH 9.0) in a total volume of 1 mL. The reaction was incubated at 20 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the absorbance readings were taken at the wavelength of 520 nm by using a microplate reader. One unit (U) of arginase enzyme activity is defined as the amount of arginase required to produce 1 μmol of urea at pH 9.0, 20 °C in a minute. For each arginase activity measurement, crude extract from uninduced E. coli host was used as negative controls to subtract the effect of the background. All enzyme assays were performed in triplicates.

Acrylic microspheres (AMS) were synthesized by emulsion photo-polymerization process according to published protocols by Ulianas et al. [15]. Briefly, a mixture of 7 mL n-butyl acrylate (nBA) monomer, 0.01 g sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) surfactant, 450 μL 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate (HDDA) cross linker, 0.1 g 2,2-dimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (DMPP) photo-initiator, 6 mg N-acryloxysuccinimide (NAS) monomer and 15 mL ultrapure water was sonicated for 10 min. The resulting emulsion solution was then photocured at 600 s with UV light under continuous nitrogen gas flow. The as-synthesized acrylic-N-acryloxysuccinimidecopolymer microspheres were then collected by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 30 min, and washed three times with 20 mM K-phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) followed by air dried at ambient conditions (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of starch-based fluorescent carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent C-dots from Dioscorea hispida (D. hispida) starch nanocrystals were synthesized as previously described [16]. The Starch nanocrystals were prepared from native Gadong tuber based on Kim et al. [17] method via acid hydrolysis procedure in aqueous sulphuric acid solution. The starch nanoparticles were then converted into carbon nanoparticles based on Chin et al. [18] method by dehydration process with concentrated sulfuric acid. In brief, 2 g of starch nanoparticles were dispersed in 5 mL of ultrapure water followed by the addition of 8 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 40 min followed by the addition of 40 mL of ultrapure water. The black carbon nanoparticles produced was collected by centrifugation at 20,000 rpm for 20 min and washed three times with ultrapure water. The black carbon nanoparticles obtained was later underwent surface oxidation reaction by dispersing them in 20 mL of 2 M nitric acid, and the mixture was refluxed for 1 h. The resulting solution was neutralized by sodium hydroxide at room temperature.The C-dots particles was collected by solvent exchangedwith anhydrous acetone and dried under nitrogen gas.

The pH sensitivity of fluorescent C-dots was studied by using 20 mM K-phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) added with different volumes of 0.1 M NaOH to produce a series of K-phosphate buffer solutions with different pHs (i.e. pH 8.0, pH 8.5, pH 9.0, pH 9.5, pH 10.0, pH 10.5 and pH 11.0). The C-dots powder was dissolved in ultrapure water to the concentration of 0.050 mg μL−1, and 80 μL of C-dots solution was dispensed into the respective K-phosphate buffers at different pHs. Fluorescence intensity of C-dots solutions in different pH media were then measured under 380 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-dots-based enzymatic fluorescence arginine biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence arginine biosensor was fabricated by first depositing 20 mg of AMS on a microscope glass slide measures at 4.6 cm by 1.0 cm, and manually compressed by using another glass slide of the same dimensions in order to get a flat surface of the immobilization matrix on the glass slide. 20 μL of 0.050 mg μL−1 C-dots solution was then deposited on the AMS glass slide and dried at room temperature. 20 μL of each urease and arginase were later carefully smeared over the C-dots-modified AMS surface, and left to dry overnight at 25 °C to allow covalent bonds to form between succinimide functional groups of AMS and amine functional groups of enzymes. The biosensing system on the glass slide was rinsed with abundant 20 mM K-phosphate buffer at pH 8.0 to remove loosely bound elements before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of C-dots loading, response time and linear response range
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of C-dots loading on the arginine biosensor response was carried out by preparing different concentrations of C-dots in ultrapure water at 0.005 mg μL−1, 0.010 mg μL−1, 0.050 mg μL−1 and 0.500 mg μL−1. 20 μL of the respective C-dots solutions at varying concentrations were then deposited on different AMS glass slides and dried at 25 °C. A total volume of 40 μL enzyme solution containing urease and arginase was deposited on the C-dots-modified AMS surface, and air-dried for another 24 h. The enzymatic biosensor labeled with C-dots pH fluorescence indicator was washed with copious amount of 20 mM K-phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) before loaded with 20 μL of 10 mM arginine solution, and left for 10 min for reaction before fluorescence measurement was taken at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

Response time of C-dots-based arginine biosensor was determined by measuring the fluorescence intensity between 1 min and 30 min reaction time in the presence of 20 μL of 10 mM arginine at pH 8.0 and 380 nm excitation wavelength. Dynamic linear response range of the fluorescence biosensor was assessed by preparing a series of arginine solutions in the concentration range of 1–100 mM using 20 mM K-phosphate buffer at pH 8.0. 20 μL of each arginine solution was deposited over the C-dots-doped enzymatic biosensor, and allowed to react for 10 min before fluorescence measurement was recorded at 420 nm emission wavelength with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. This experimentwasperformedintriplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Arginine biosensor’s long term stability and interference studies
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate arginine biosensor long term stability performance, 60 units of arginine biosensors based on AMS enzyme supporting matrix and C-dots pH label were fabricated and kept in the freezer (−20 °C). 3 units of biosensors were taken out of the freezer and reacted with 20 μL of 10 mM arginine solution prior to fluorescence measurement was carried out at excitation wavelength of 380 nm. This experiment was repeated every day until a no fluorescence signal was detected.

Interference study was performed by using a variety of foreign compounds such as lysine, glutamic acid, alanine, aspartic acid, serine and citrulline. 20 μL of these potentially interfering substances in 20 mM K-phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) were deposited separately onto different individual enzymatic biosensors modified with C-dots pH label, and allowed to react for 10 min. Fluorescence measurement was taken at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and compared with the fluorescence intensity obtained for C-dots-modified bi-enzyme biosensor without the presence of foreign substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of arginine concentration in fruit juices with C-dots-labeled bi-enzyme fluorescence microsensor and validation with HPLC standard method
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh fruitssuch as grapes, mangoes and oranges without any apparent physical damage were purchased from a local supermarketin Kajang, Selangor, Malaysia, andwere usedfor preparing thejuiceunder laboratory conditions. Three types of fruit juices’ samples have been squeezeddirectly from grapes, mangoes and oranges using handpresskitchensqueeze tool, and filtered into separate glass bottles using sieve and Whatman filter paper Grade 1 before being adjusted to pH 8.0 by adding 0.1 M NaOH solution. 20 μL of the respective fruit juice samples were then dispensed on the C-dots-labeled microspheres surface of the enzymatic biosensor, and fluorescence intensity at 420 nm was measured at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm after 10 min of reaction time.

The same fruit juice samples were analyzed by isocratic reversed-phase high performance liquid chromatography (RP-HPLC) method after being filtered through 0.45 µm syringe filter for quantitation of arginine concentration. 0.1% Phosphoric acid (H3P04) in ultrapure water was used as mobile phase with a flow rate of 0.5 mL min−1. C18 column (150 mm × 4.6 mm; 2.6 μm) was used as stationary phase. The column temperature was fixed at room temperature and UV–Vis detection was performed at 195 nm. Statistical t-studenttest was applied tocomparethe average arginine concentration values of twodatasets obtained by biosensor and HPLC standard method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of recombinant arginase and enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: Arginase from G. antarctica (LAN_05_105) was used as the targeted enzyme as it was a well characterized active enzyme in our laboratory (unpublished data). It is an enzyme that requires no elevated temperature for enzyme activity hence more cost effective to be used as a biosensor. E. coli expression system was used for recombinant arginase production as the bacteria is easy to be cultured and manipulated [19]. The arginase from G. antarctica (GaArg) was successfully expressed as an active soluble enzyme using E. coli BL21 (DE3) expression host. SDS-PAGE profile showed that GaArg recombinant protein with a molecular weight of ~32 kDa was overexpressed using LB broth with the supplementation of 0.1 mM IPTG incubated at 16 °C for 18 h (Fig. A.1, lane 5 in the Supplementary Material). The presence of other protein bands observed with similar molecular weight in the uninduced transformant (Fig. A.1, lane 4 in the Supplementary Material) could be due to the existence of other endogenous intracellular proteins from E. coli that possess similar molecular weight. Similar observation is also shown in the crude extracts from the uninduced and induced E. coli host (Fig. A.1 lane 2 and 3 in the Supplementary Material), indicating that these endogenous proteins were produced during the basal expression level in the E. coli. Alternatively, leaky protein expression can happen when T7 lac promoter is not tightly regulated in the bacterial strain and could be prevented by using E. coli pLysS strain. This strain expresses T7 lysozyme to tightly bind T7 lac promoter prior to IPTG induction or adding glucose into the growth media that will repress the promoter before protein induction [20].

The production of active recombinant arginase enzyme was further verified by arginase activity assay. The arginase activity was determined quantitatively by using arginine as the substrate. The arginase enzyme assay was performed by mixing 10 µg mL−1 crude GaArg with 10 mM arginine in 15 mM glycine-NaOH buffer (pH 9.0) and incubated at 20 °C for 30 min. The absorption of the reaction mixture was measured at 520 nm by means of UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Crude extract from the uninduced E. coli host was used as the negative control and assayed under the same condition. Recombinant crude arginase activities (U mL−1) were determined based on urea standard curve in Fig. A.2 (see Supplementary Material). Based on the enzyme activity assays, GaArg was successfully expressed and catalytically active with total activity of 30 mU mL−1, and will be used for the development of arginine-specific biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon nanodots synthesized from natural polysaccharides
ce:section.ce:para: TEM image of carbonized starch nanocrystals is shown in Fig. 2 . In terms of diameter, the as-synthesized carbon nanodots (C-dots) from starch precursors cover a range between 6 nm and 28 nm. In comparison, the size of C-dots prepared from natural Gadong polysaccharides in this study was found to be smaller than those C-dots from sago starch prepared by Chin et al. [18]. The dehydration of starch precursors followed by surface oxidation of carbon nanoparticles have changed the surface groups of C-dots. The resultant carbon nanoparticles synthesized from starch nanocrystals by dehydration process was black in colour and completely insoluble in water as they were hydrophobic in nature, and subsequent surface oxidation reaction on the black carbon nanoparticles was then led to production of C-dots with hydrophilic nature.

The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of C-dots (Fig. A.3) synthesized from natural precursors exhibited no distinctive diffraction peak confirms that the sample is an amorphous state. The starch-based fluorescent C-dots synthesized by Chin et al. [18] has exhibited the similar XRD pattern. The UV absorption spectrum of C-dots (Fig. A.4), which dynthesized from three different natural tuber samples showed a reproducible absorbance peak at 290 nm with relative standard deviation (RSD) acquired at 0.3%, which is similar to the absorption peak of luminescent carbogenic dots reported by Peng and Sejdic [21] and fluorescent C-dots at 299 nm by Chin et al. [18]. The physico-chemical properties of the Gadong starch-based C-dots was highly constant even it was prepared from different natural precursors with different preparations, which can be proven bylow RSD value that implies high-reproducibilityC-dots weresynthesized. The UV absorption by C-dots is typically ascribed to the n → π* electron transition associated with CO functional group. The fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots is demonstrated in Fig. A.5 in the in supplementary data. The c-dots samples presented broad fluorescence band with maximum emission wavelength registered at 441 nm with three different excitation wavelengths at 260 nm, 290 nm and 320 nm were applied.

The effect of pH on the as-synthesized C-dots was attempted in the alkaline conditions, and the fluorescence intensity of C-dots towards changes of pH is presented in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity of C-dots at 420 nm was noticed to be pH dependent, whereby the fluorescence intensity of C-dots increased as increasing the pH from pH 8.0 to pH 10.0, after which the fluorescence intensity declined from pH 10.5 to pH 11.0. The average reproducibility RSD from triplicate fluorescence measurement of C-dots between pH 8.0 and pH 11.0 was obtained at 0.5%. The small RSD value indicates high reproducibility fluorescence performance of C-dots under varying pH conditions. The enhancement of fluorescence intensity between pH 8.0 and pH 10.0 was attributed to deprotonation of both carboxylic and phenolic moieties of C-dots as high alkalinity had led to doping of C-dots to occur, and created more surface defects on C-dots that ultimately protected them against fluorescence quenching by dissolved oxygen molecules [22]. In addition, by increasing the pH medium towards basic conditions, it reduced non-radiation transition process, and resulted in the heightening of C-dots’ fluorescent emission [23]. This phenomenon has been reported by Mandal et al. [24] that an increase in pH has increased the room temperature-phosphorescence (RTP). However, higherpH (i.e. > pH 10.0) was inclined to quench C-dots’ fluorescence emission resultingfromchanges in the surface charge inducedby protonation–deprotonation reaction. The significant decrement in fluorescence intensities at higher pH surrounding could also be due to the formation of hydrated products and lower solubility of C-dots [25]. Since a dramatic increment of fluorescence intensity was attained between pH 8.0 and pH 10.0, it signifies high sensitivity of the fluorescent C-dots derived from natural polysaccharides towards pH, which can be applied in the subsequent fluorescence sensing of arginine based on enzymatic biosensor system with immobilized C-dots as potential pH fluorescent label.

The FESEM images of unmodified acrylic microspheres (AMS), C-dots-modified AMS and enzymes-immobilized AMS with pH sensitive C-dots label are shown in Fig. A.6, Fig. A.7 and Fig. A.8, respectively in the in supplementary data. The FESEM image (Fig. A.6) shows spherical morphology with smooth surface of AMS with diameters around 0.50 μm to 1.80 μm, and 1.20 μm as the dominant size. The microspherical shape of AMS provided a large surface area to volume ratio, which is essential in promoting high loading capacity for both C-dots and enzyme molecules. AMS microparticles stained with C-dots showed rather rough surface (Fig. A.7), and further covalent binding with arginase and urease, a streak surface on the AMS spheres was generated (Fig. A.8). The C-dots labeled bienzymatic biosensing system based on AMS showed green fluorescence rays after exposed to UV light at 365 nm as compared to when it was exposed to ordinary white light (Fig. A.9). This confirmed that the brightly fluorescent Gadong starch-based C-dots have been successfully adsorbed on the polyacrylate microspheres to produce a new type of optical sensing probe.

The FTIR spectra of starch-based fluorescent C-dots and AMS microparticles is illustrated in Fig. A.10 in the supplementary data. AMS is composed of repeating units of monomers known as n-butyl acrylate (C7H12O2) in a long polymer chain. FTIR spectrum for AMS depicts sp 3 absorption peak for CH stretch (2690 cm−1), CH2 bend (1458 cm−1), CH3 bend (1384 cm−1), CO stretch (1734 cm−1), CO stretch (1164 cm−1) and CC alkene strain (1639 cm−1) that comes along with CH out of plane bending vibration in the wavenumber range of 1000–650 cm−1 [26]. The FTIR spectrum of C-dots, on the other hand, exhibits a broad band at 3433 cm−1 that assigned to the OH stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1682 cm−1 is corresponds to the carbonyl (CO) stretching vibration [27]. The typical characteristic absorption peaks of polysaccharide sample can be found between wavenumber of 500 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 with specific peak positions at 2930 cm−1, 1130 cm−1, 1104 cm−1, 997 cm−1, 925 cm−1 and 832 cm−1, whereby 832 cm−1 is attributed to the IR absorption of α-glucose [28]. And, in this study, absorption peak at 829 cm−1 indicates the presence of aromatic CH stretch owing to the natural polysaccharide precursor was used in this experiment. Other two prominent IR absorption peaks at 2092 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 are correspond to the sp 3 CH stretch and aliphatic CH bending vibrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dots concentration, arginine detection time and dynamic linear range
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain high sensitivity of the arginine biosensor performance based on pH sensitive fluorophore C-dots, different concentrations of C-dots have been loaded on the biosensing platform, and photoluminescence spectra of C-dots-based enzymatic arginine biosensor under 380 nm excitation were investigated. As Fig. 4 shows, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor at 420 nm increased by increasing the C-dots loading from 0.005 mg μL−1 to 0.050 mg μL−1. However, when further increased the C-dots concentration to 0.500 mg μL−1 loaded on the AMS surface, the intensity of immobilized C-dots considerably decreased. Blank AMS and enzymes-modified AMS without the presence of C-dots were used as negative controls that did not show fluorescence emission. Attenuation of fluorescence intensity at high loading of C-dots on the AMS matrix can be attributed to the aggregation of C-dot particles, whereby particles with larger sizes were formed on the substrate due to coalescence of carbon-based QDs, which led to loss of fluorescence [29]. As such, C-dots with a concentration of 0.050 mg μL−1 was selected as the optimum loading to be applied as pH labeling agent for indirect monitoring of arginine concentration in the next experiments.

The reaction time taken between arginine and luminescent C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor will determine the analysis time for bioassay of arginine concentration [30]. The fluorescence response of the enzymatic biosensor over a duration of 1 min to 30 min reaction time is demonstrated in Fig. A.11. An increasing fluorescence response trend towards sensing of 10 mM arginine at the beginning was perceived between 1 min and 10 min due to increasing enzymatic reaction rates of immobilized arginase that catalyzed the hydrolyzing of arginine into ornithine and urea, followed by enzymatic hydrolysis of urea by immobilized urease to produce the corresponding ammonium hydroxide andbicarbonate,resulting in an elevated pH at the biosensor-solution interface, and that the intensity of immobilized C-dots was heightened, as a result of deprotonation of immobilized C-dots. After 10 min of reaction time, there was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity of the C-dots-based arginine biosensor. This steady state can be regarded as the rate of formation of enzymes-substrate complex that is equal to its breakdown rate, and yielding of plateau response curve [31,32]. Therefore, arginine biosensing reaction was maintained at 10 min before each fluorescence measurement was taken.

The fluorescence enzymatic biosensor doped with Gadong starch-based C-dots has been employed to detect various arginine concentration between 1 mM and 100 mM in 20 mM K-phosphate buffer (pH 8.0). As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity increased linearly with arginine concentration over a range of 1 mM to 10 mM ascribed to the increasing localized pH at C-dots-modified AMS as a result of increasing bienzymatic reaction rate, which had increased the amount of hydroxide ions being generated, thereby promoting deprotonation reaction rate at the carboxyl functional groups of C-dots. As the arginine concentration varied between 20 mM and 100 mM, the fluorescence biosensor response gradually increased, and reached a plateau when the enzyme active sites have been saturated with substrates. At this stage, the rate of reaction has reached a maximum with no further increase at a particular substrate concentration. The linear response range of the arginine biosensor from 1 to 10 mM gave a reproducible photoluminescence response with a RSD obtained at 4.8%. The dynamic linear detection range of the fluorescence arginine biosensor falls well within the typical arginine levels in grape and other fruit juices (i.e. <6 mM), which is well suited for application to determine arginine inboth fresh and commercial fruit juices.

The biosensor limit of detection (LOD) is defined by the lowest concentration of arginine in which no changes in biosensor fluorescence response was calculated according to the equation LOD = 3 s/m, where ‘s’ is the average of standard deviation of seven blanks and ‘m’ is the slope of calibration curve [33], which was obtained at 0.9 mM arginine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The lifetime and selectivity of luminescent C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The bienzymatic biosensors were stored in the freezer at −20 °C in order to prevent denaturation of the protein, which could result in a loss of enzyme activity. Enzymes will denature if they are warmed up or stored at room temperature for extended periods of time. Therefore, the enzymes in immobilized state were kept cold throughout the experimental storage period. Based on long term stability study of the C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor towards fluorescence detection of 10 mM arginine, the optical biosensor shows stable response at maximum fluorescence intensity for the first 5 days of experimental time period at 420 nm emission wavelength (Fig. A.12). The C-dots-based arginine biosensor, however, was still capable to remain about 90% of its initial fluorescence intensity on day-7, and the biosensor fluorescence response slowly declined with time thereafter until reaching zero fluorescence response on the 18th day of experiment. The diminishing fluorescence response with time might be due to degradation of biosensing components (i.e. enzymes) during prolonged storage in the freezer. Furthermore, the effect ofprolonged storage may also degrade the immobilized C-dot particles, which are generally sensitive towards changes of temperature [34]. Therefore, the proposed arginine biosensing system based on C-dots-labeled bienzymatic AMS has a life expectancy of about a week.

Commercial beverages especially fruit juices contain several interfering substances that may interfere with the photoluminescence response of C-dots pH probe of the enzymatic biosensor towards sensing of arginine concentration. Pre-packed fruit juice is basically a mixture of carbohydrates, fats and fatty acids, vitamins, minerals, proteins and amino acids. The six main amino acids that are typically present in the packaged fruit juices are such as lysine, glutamic acid, alanine, aspartic acid, serine and arginine [16]. Nonetheless, the amount of these potential amino acids varied according to the type of fruit juices, which are produced on a commercial basis. As shown in Fig. A.13, only arginine showed remarkable luminescence signal with the developed enzymatic biosensor doped with Gadong starch-based C-dots as pH fluorescence label compared with other potentially interfering foreign substances. This suggests high selectivity of the C-dots–based biosensor against bioassay of arginine concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of fluorescence enzymatic microbiosensor based on C-dots pH labeling agent for arginine concentration quantification in fresh fruit juices
ce:section.ce:para: Arginine is one of the most common amino acids in fruit juices especially in grape juice, which is closely related to ethyl carbamate levels in wine [2,35]. Ethyl carbamate is also known as urethane, which occurs naturally in wine and some other food stuffs and beverages, and has been classified as carcinogenic to humans [36,37]. In connection with this, an attempt has been made to evaluate the accuracy and applicability of the optimized fluorescence arginine biosensor for screening of arginine concentration in three types of fruit juices. The results obtained with the C-dots-based fluorescence enzymatic microbiosensor have been statistically compared with HPLC method using the t-studenttest. The concentrations of arginine in squeezed fruit juices such as mangoes, grapes and oranges determined by both biosensor and HPLC methods are tabulated in Table 1 . It is interesting to note that both biosensor and HPLC reference methods show the highest arginine concentration in grape juice followed by orange juice and mango juice. The small t values acquired for three types of fruit juice samples indicate no statistically significant difference between the arginine concentrations determined by both method at 95% confidence level, and that the proposed C-dots-labeled bi-enzyme fluorescence microsensor performance is comparable with HPLC standard method in determining the concentration of arginine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of analytical performance of carbon dots-labeled enzymatic biosensor with other reported arginine biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of a sensor or biosensor is defined by a few characteristics such as speed of analysis, linear response range, stability, miniaturization, sensitivity, limit of detection, etc. as some of the useful figures of merit (FOM) [2,3]. Table 2 summarizes the FOM of some optical and electrochemical platforms of arginine biosensors fabricated with different methods reported in the contemporary literature [33,38–40]. The C-dots-based enzymatic biosensor turned up to be highly selective towards arginine detection compared to the use of 8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonic acid trisodium salt (HPTS) as a colorimetric and green turn-on fluorescent probe for arginine recognition, which is vulnerable to lysine interference during target analyte detection is performed in aqueous solution [38]. Arginine deiminase co-immobilized with ZnS QDs coupled micro-disk, which was developed by Verma et al. [40], was found to be highly sensitive towards phosphorescence detection of arginine. However, the sensitivity of arginine sensor/biosensor fabricated based on HPTS pH fluorophore and ZnS QDs was limited to the low-level assay of arginine at <1 mM. The proposed carbon-based QDs bienzyme fluorescence biosensor appeared to permit detection of arginine content in real sample without spiking of standard analyte or the need of pre-dilution of fruit juice sample. This eliminates the pre-treatment step to dilute fruit juice sample prior to signal measurement is carried out. Although amperometric biosensor based on polyaniline-nafion-modified electrode showed rapid response, within seconds towards arginine determination, however, the electrochemical biosensing system is susceptible to interference from canavanine, i.e. the substrate of arginase I that induces an essential analytical signal slightly higher than arginine, and poor stability of three days operational duration [39].

Bi-enzyme biosensor for arginine determination has been developed by exploiting the unique biorecognition capacities of two enzymes of urea cycle-arginase and urease. pH sensitive C-dots were prepared from native Gadong starch, and applied as pH labeling agent as part of the fluorescence bienzymatic biosensor based on acrylic microspheres solid supports. The enzymatic biosensor demonstrated reproducible photoluminescence detection of arginine with reproducibility RSD of <5.0%, which indicates the fabrication method can typically provide reproducible fluorescence bi-enzyme arginine biosensor. The fluorescence intensity increased as increasing the pH reaction medium as a result of deprotonation of both thecarboxylicand phenolicfunctional groups of C-dots, which revealed a good sensitivity of starch-based C-dots towards changes of pH, and functionalized as effective fluorescent pH probes. In addition, the developed arginine biosensor based on brightly luminescent C-dots from natural polysaccharides is cost-effective as it involved very small amount of biological and chemical components for its fabrication.

Regina Sisika A. Sonthanasamy: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Azwan Mat Lazim: Project administration. Siti Nur Syazni Mohd Zuki: Visualization, Methodology. Doris Huai Xia Quay: Formal analysis, Validation. Ling Ling Tan: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National University of Malaysia (UKM) via Research University Grant [GUP-2018-156]. We would like to acknowledge Assoc. Prof. Dr. Abdul Munir Abd Murad for providing pET30 Ek/LIC plasmid harbouring Glaciozyma antarctica arginase gene (GaArg). We would also like to express our gratitude towards UKM Centre for Research and Instrumentation Management (CRIM) for the support of analytical instrument services.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2020.106345.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
